# Italy Trip Recap — October 2025

Ten days: Rome, Florence, and a Chianti farmhouse. Written up for
next-time-us and for friends who keep asking for the list.

## Rome (3 nights)

Stayed in Monti, which remains the correct answer — walkable to the Forum
before the crowds and full of wine bars that don't care about your dinner
reservation. The Borghese Gallery's timed entry was the best ticket of the
trip; book the 9am slot weeks ahead. Skipped the Vatican museums this time
on purpose, zero regrets.

Food notes: the carbonara at the little place on Via dei Serpenti beat the
famous one we queued 40 minutes for in Trastevere. Supplì over arancini,
always. The Testaccio market lunch (cacio e pepe sandwich, stall 15) was
the single best cheap meal of the trip.

## Florence (3 nights)

The Duomo climb is overrated when the Campanile next door has the better
view — of the dome itself — with a third of the line. Uffizi on a Tuesday
afternoon was shockingly calm. Day trip to Siena by bus (not train —
the bus drops you at the top of the hill) for the striped cathedral and
the best gelato of the trip at a place we couldn't find again the same day.

A leather jacket was acquired in the San Lorenzo back streets after
exactly the negotiation ritual everyone describes. No regrets, worn since.

## Chianti farmhouse (3 nights)

Rented a car in Florence (automatic transmission booked three months out —
they really do run out). The farmhouse outside Radda had an honor-system
wine cellar and a pool nobody used in October. Two winery visits is the
right number per day; three is a hangover with scenery. The Tuesday market
in Greve supplied every picnic.

Driving notes: the ZTL camera zones in Florence are real and the rental
company forwards the fines with a service fee — park at Villa Costanza
park-and-ride and tram in, it's 20 minutes and saves both money and
marriage.

## Verdicts

Best meal: the farmhouse dinner the host cooked on the last night.
Biggest surprise: Siena over Florence for atmosphere.
Do differently: four nights Rome, two Florence. Rome wins on every axis
except museum density.
